policy_name: Donor and Recipient Blood Identification Policy
purpose: This policy describes how <<LAB_NAME>> verifies positive identification of both the donor unit and the recipient at component issue and transfusion, the single most important control against ABO-mismatch transfusion. Required by 21 CFR 606.140, 21 CFR 606.151, and 21 CFR 630.15.

cfr_text_blocks.label: FDA: Compatibility testing (specimen ID)
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: A method of collecting and identifying the blood samples of recipients to ensure positive identification.

scope: All <<LAB_NAME>> transfusion service personnel and all clinical staff administering blood components.
policy_statements: At component issue from the transfusion service, the issuing technologist verifies: patient name and second identifier on the issue slip match the unit's patient assignment tag; ABO/Rh on the unit matches the patient's compatible result; expiration is current; visual inspection shows no abnormality.

At the bedside immediately before transfusion, two qualified clinical staff independently verify the patient identity against the unit's patient assignment tag and the component label (ABO/Rh, unit number, expiration); both staff sign the bedside check.
If any element fails verification, the transfusion is NOT started; the component is returned to the transfusion service and the discrepancy is investigated.
All identification verifications (issue slip, bedside check) are documented and retained for at least 10 years per 42 CFR 493.1105(a)(7).
procedure_steps: At issue, the technologist completes the issue slip with patient identifiers and unit identifiers; both are verified visually against the unit label and the crossmatch record.
At the bedside, two clinical staff independently verify patient against unit per the bedside check procedure; both sign.
Any discrepancy halts the transfusion and triggers transfusion service notification.
definitions: Two-staff independent verification of patient identity against the unit immediately before transfusion start. The most critical control against ABO-mismatch.
